retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Glutamate is needed for factor IX production, and vitamin E reduces glutamate production. [3] In patients who take warfarin and vitamin E, vitamin K antagonism is increased, thus increasing their risk of bleeding. Platelet aggregation was found to be decreased in patients taking vitamin E supplementation. The exact mechanism is unknown but is thought to be related to protein kinase C inhibition, which is linked to the inhibition of platelet aggregation. [14] Through these mechanisms, it is easy to see why vitamin E can be associated with higher risks for bleeding, especially in patients taking oral anticoagulants or antiplatelet medications.  “Toxic” vitamin E levels do not concentrate in the body in the same fashion as other fat-soluble vitamins like vitamins A and D. [12] Although vitamin E is a lipid-soluble vitamin, excess alpha-tocopherols are excreted in the urine and bile, thereby decreasing the accumulation of circulating alpha-tocopherols. [12] As a result, toxic effects are not observed in most healthy patients taking less than 1000 mg of vitamin E daily. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Fundamentals -- Sources of Vitamin E
retrieved_docs.content: The predominant form of vitamin E is a-tocopherol. However, other tocopherols and tocotrienols are also in circulation, such as the alpha, gamma, beta, and delta forms. Naturally occurring sources of vitamin E include vegetable oils, seeds, nuts, and whole grains. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin E (Tocopherol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin E is best known to function as a chain-breaking antioxidant that neutralizes the lipid peroxyl radicals during lipid peroxidation to prevent cyclic propagation of lipid peroxidation. This process is key in protecting the polyunsaturated fatty acids within the phospholipids of plasma membranes and plasma lipoproteins. [53] Vitamin K (P hylloquinone; Menaquinone)

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Testing -- Vitamin E (Tocopherol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin E status is difficult to test because serum concentrations of vitamin E are largely age-dependent and are influenced by blood lipids. In the general population, α-tocopherol plasma levels can range from 19.9 micromoles/L to 34.2 micromoles. More research needs to be done to adjust specific requirements to an individual's bioavailability. [67] Vitamin K (P hylloquinone; Menaquinone)

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: The primary mechanism of action of alpha-tocopherol is as a scavenger of free radicals. It binds to free radicals, protecting cellular components from oxidative damage. Peroxidation of polyunsaturated fatty acids in the cell membrane from free radicals leads to cellular injury. Vitamin E plays a protective role. It also inhibits the aggregation of platelets, monocyte adhesion, and proliferation of cells. [1] [4] Vitamin E is hepatically metabolized and is primarily excreted in the bile and feces but also the urine. It is also stored in the body's adipose tissues.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Pathophysiology -- Vitamin E
retrieved_docs.content: Hypervitaminosis E is most commonly a result of over-supplementation and is otherwise very rare. Since high doses of Vitamin E (800 mg per day) inhibit platelet aggregation, it is contraindicated in patients on anticoagulants. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E Toxicity -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: In a study performed on patients with intracranial hemorrhages and taking vitamin E supplementation, alpha-tocopherol levels ranged from 23.3 micromoles/L to 46.7 micromoles/L in patients who were discovered to have intracranial hemorrhages. [3] In another study that correlated the vitamin E levels and risk of bleeding in patients taking oral anticoagulation, a ratio of circulating alpha-tocopherols to total serum cholesterol concentration was used. This was thought to represent the true circulating vitamin E levels most accurately. [5] Although there is a concern regarding the reliability of these circulating alpha-tocopherol levels correlating to vitamin E levels, this is still the most widely used test in the literature regarding quantifying vitamin E when describing its effects.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The provider must remember that serum alpha-tocopherol depends on serum lipids and proteins. Therefore, in patients with high total lipid levels, the measurement of serum alpha-tocopherol is best estimated by dividing the level by the total lipid. Additionally, since vitamin E and other fat-soluble vitamins require carrier proteins, those with other hypoproteinemic states may have lower measurements of such vitamins.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the mechanism of action of vitamin E. Describe the potential adverse effects of vitamin E. Review the appropriate monitoring when patients are taking vitamin E. Summarize interprofessional team strategies for improving care coordination and communication to advance vitamin E treatment and improve outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the etiology of vitamin E deficiency. Outline the presentation of a patient with vitamin E deficiency. Review the treatment options for vitamin E deficiency. Explain the importance of improving coordination among the interprofessional team to enhance the delivery of care for patients affected by vitamin E deficiency. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E Toxicity -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the foods that contain vitamin E along with the reasons why patients take vitamin E supplementation. Assess the pathophysiology of vitamin E toxicity. Evaluate the complications that occur due to vitamin E toxicity. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Pathophysiology -- Vitamin E
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin-E deficiency is extremely rare and principally occurs in individuals with fat malabsorption, abetalipoproteinemia (defect in microsomal transfer protein), and hypobetalipoproteinemia (mutation in apolipoprotein B) disorders. Deficiency symptoms include limb and truncal ataxia, hyporeflexia, and upward gaze limitations. Rarer manifestations are muscle weakness and constriction of visual fields. If left untreated, deficiency can result in blindness, memory impairment, and arrhythmias. [13] Multiple clinical trials have shown that vitamin-E supplementation decreases histological and biochemical evidence of liver dysfunction in patients with nonalcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD) and nonalcoholic steatohepatitis (NASH). The increased inflammation and oxidative stress observed in obesity theoretically heighten vitamin E requirements in this subset of patients. Additionally, these findings raise whether vitamin E deficiency could exacerbate liver dysfunction. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Fat Soluble Vitamins -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin K is necessary to activate certain clotting factors in the liver responsible for coagulation. For activation to occur, the clotting proteins must bind calcium. Vitamin K-dependent gamma-carboxylation of certain glutamic acid residues allows the proteins to bind calcium and carry out the coagulation cascade. Specifically, vitamin K serves as a cofactor for gamma-glutamyl carboxylase and catalyzes the post-translational synthesis of gamma-carboxy-glutamyl residues. This process activates prothrombin and factors VII, IX, X, protein C, and S. Oxidation of vitamin K hydroquinone supplies energy for these carboxylation reactions. Regeneration of vitamin K hydroquinone relies on vitamin K epoxide reductase and vitamin K quinone reductase.

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Clinical Significance -- Vitamin E (Tocopherol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin E deficiency is very rare but can occur in certain individuals. Symptoms of vitamin E deficiency include oxidative damage of tissues, membrane damage of cells, neurological abnormalities such as peripheral neuropathy, muscular, functional abnormalities like ataxia, and hemolytic anemia. Vitamin E deficiency most commonly occurs in premature babies of very low birthweight, people with fat-malabsorption disorders like Crohn’s disease and cystic fibrosis, and those who have a rare neurodegenerative disease called ataxia with vitamin E deficiency (AVED) that is caused by mutations in the gene for αβ-tocopherol transfer protein. [75] Vitamin K (P hylloquinone; Menaquinone)

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin E Toxicity -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Since an increased risk of bleeding is the major concern with vitamin E toxicity, a thorough medication history must be obtained, including any anticoagulation or antiplatelet agents. The history should also include any over-the-counter supplements with exact dosages, as patients may be taking many available supplements at their discretion. The history of weight loss or weight gain may also be important when considering the malabsorption of fat-soluble vitamins. With vitamin E toxicity, other fat-soluble vitamins can be decreased. [12] Some of the major drugs that vitamin E can interact with can cause complications with increased bleeding, hepatobiliary dysfunction, and malabsorption of other fat-soluble vitamins. Prescription medication interactions can occur when vitamin E levels are supplemented at levels greater than 300 mg daily. These common medications include aspirin, warfarin, cyclosporine, and tamoxifen. [15] Physical findings should include a thorough skin exam for bruising, petechiae, and pallor. Important findings for hepatobiliary dysfunction include scleral icterus, hepatomegaly, and jaundice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 44
retrieved_docs.content: Multivitamin preparations, multivitamin agents, and multivitamins with minerals are grouped under the broad vitamin category and are used to prevent nutritional deficiency. Vitamin IV solution additives contain fat‑soluble vitamins such as vitamin K (subtypes K1, K2, K3) and retinoids; retinoids serve as the parent entity of vitamin A, while plain vitamin A belongs to a combination subclass that also includes vitamin A + D formulations. Alpha‑tocopherol and tocopherol are individual members of the vitamin E family, and hydroxocobalamin is a specific form of vitamin B12. Vitamin B12 also includes cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, and is combined with folic acid in antianemic preparations (e.g., folic‑acid 1 mg / vitamin B12 0.5 mg oral tablet) for anemia therapy. Vitamin B1 (plain thiamine) is used alone or in combination products with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12. The vitamin D family comprises cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃) and its metabolites calcifediol and calcitriol,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 566
retrieved_docs.content: Benzopyrany serves as a parent chemical class that includes vitamin E as a subordinate entity, indicating that vitamin E is structurally related to the benzopyrany family. In addition, vitamin E is listed as a member of the ATC category “other plain vitamin preparations,” placing it among other single‑ingredient vitamin products in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical classification system. This dual classification underscores vitamin E’s identity both as a benzopyrany‑derived compound and as a standard plain vitamin preparation used in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 206
retrieved_docs.content: The community organizes “vitaminy” as a central classification that unites individual vitamins (α‑tocopherol/α‑tocoferol, cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃), calcitriol, vitamin K, vitamin K₂, vitamin K₃, vitamin C/ascorbic acid, vitamin A, vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂, folic acid, β‑carotene, thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxocobalamin, hydroxokobalamin, retinoids, cholin) and their complexes, precursors, and analogues (e.g., dihydrotachysterol, retinoidy). These entities are hierarchically linked to broader groups such as fat‑soluble vitamins (parent of vitamin K and retinoids), steroid hormones (including the active vitamin D metabolite calcitriol and cholecalciferol), and the “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors” category. Multivitamin agents, multivitamin preparations, and multivitamin + mineral preparations are positioned within the vitaminy framework, exemplified by the prenatal multivitamin product (VITAMIN A acetate

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 121
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a comprehensive ontology of vitamins, minerals, and related pharmaceutical agents. Multivitamin + mineral preparations and prenatal multivitamin formulations integrate numerous individual vitamins—α‑tocopherol (alpha‑tocopherol), its variant tocopheroly, bioflavonoids, cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃), calcitriol, dihydrotachysterol, kalcitriol, ubiquinone, vitamin K (K₂, K₃), retinoids, ascorbic acid (vitamin C), vitamin A (plain and acetate), vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxokobalamin and analogues), folic acid, and the broader vitamin B complex, together with minerals such as calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide. Hierarchical relationships place hydroxokobalamin and the combined vitamin B₁₂/folic‑acid group as parent elements of the vitamin set; vitamin B₁ (alone or with B₆ and B₁₂) is an inverse subclass. Cholin is a child of the vitamin class, while retinoids are positioned as a parent of

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 139
retrieved_docs.content: The knowledge base defines **vitaminy** as an umbrella category that encompasses the individual vitamins A, D, E, K and the B‑complex (thiamine B₁, pyridoxine B₆, cyanocobalamin B₁₂ and its analogues hydroxokobalamin and mecobalamin, and folic acid), their specific chemical forms (α‑tocopherol, tokoferols, cholecalciferol, vitamin D₂, calcitriol, dihydrotachysterol, retinoids, vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, etc.), and a wide range of combination products such as multivitamin preparations, vitamin IV solution additives, multivitamin + mineral formulations, prenatal tablets, oral multivitamin‑with‑iron tablets, and IV solutions. Within this hierarchy vitamin K (including subtypes K₂ and K₃) is linked to both oral multivitamin‑with‑iron tablets and certain IV solution additives, while **retinoids** are positioned as a child of the vitamin A branch and also as a narrower subset of the vitamin K grouping. **Cholin** is noted as a parent of vitaminy, indicating biochemical interactions

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 93
retrieved_docs.content: The “vitaminy” class is defined as a comprehensive category that encompasses individual vitamins, their chemical variants, related cofactors, and commercial vitamin preparations. Within this hierarchy ascorbic acid (vitamin C) appears as a plain vitamin C product, as part of vitamin C combinations, and in calcium‑as‑corbate formulations; β‑tocopherol, tocopheroly, and α‑tocopherol acetate represent forms of vitamin E, which is a subclass of the benzopyrany parent class and is listed under “other plain vitamin preparations” in the ATC system. Riboflavin (vitamin B2) and vitamin B1 (thiamine) are included as plain vitamins, with vitamin B1 also occurring in combinations with vitamins B6 and B12. Vitamin B12 is represented by cyanocobalamin, hydroxocobalamin, mecobalamin and other analogues; these, together with folic acid, constitute antianemic preparations (ATC group “other antianemic preparations”) and are found in specific oral tablets (folic acid 1 mg + vitamin B12 0.5 mg) and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 129
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamins constitute a distinct class of organic compounds organized by hierarchical “isa” and parent/child relationships and serve essential nutritional and therapeutic roles. Individual members include cholecalciferol (vitamin D₃), calcitriol, vitamin D, vitamin D₂, vitamin K (K₁, K₂, K₃), vitamin A, vitamin B₁ (alone or combined with vitamin B₆ and vitamin B₁₂), the vitamin B₁₂‑folic‑acid complex, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, retinoids, cholin and related cobalamin forms (cyanocobalamin, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin). Vitamin A and vitamin D and their analogues are grouped under “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” reflecting a shared family. Multivitamin agents, such as prenatal multivitamin tablets, are classified as vitamins and contain vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: The community groups all vitamin‑related agents under the class “vitaminy,” linking them to nutritional deficiency and to a broader chemical domain. Fat‑soluble vitamins form a sub‑hierarchy that includes vitamin A (retinoids), vitamin D (cholecalciferol, kalcifediol = 25‑hydroxy‑vitamin D, and kalcitriol = 1,25‑dihydroxy‑vitamin D), vitamin E (α‑tocoferol and related tokoferols), and vitamin K, while water‑soluble vitamins comprise vitamin C (ascorbic acid) and the B‑complex (thiamine B₁, riboflavin B₂, niacinamide B₃, pyridoxine B₆, cyanocobalamin B₁₂, hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, and folic acid) together with their combination products. Multivitamin preparations are child concepts of “vitaminy” and illustrate co‑administration of multiple vitamins and essential minerals; examples include plain multivitamin tablets, liquid multivitamins such as IRON/MULTIVITAMS LIQUID, and prenatal multivitamins containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 31
retrieved_docs.content: B1 (plain)** may appear alone or with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12; **vitamin B12** includes cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin, all members of the vitamin B12 family. Fat‑soluble vitamins are also integrated into multivitamin formulas; **vitamin K** (a fat‑soluble vitamin) frequently co‑occurs with iron, while **retinoids** (the vitamin A subclass) illustrate shared solubility characteristics. In topical dermatologic products, **alpha‑tocopherol** (a form of vitamin E) functions as an inactive ingredient in zinc pyrithione medicated shampoos (e.g., formulations containing 3 mg/mL or 4.8 mg/mL zinc pyrithione), demonstrating how vitamin‑derived compounds serve as excipients that support the delivery and stability of antimicrobial agents. Collectively, these relationships illustrate the strategic combination of folic acid, iron, vitamin K, retinoids, vitamin B‑complex members, vitamin E and other micronutrients in multivitamin and topical preparations to address nutritional

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 2 -- Community 330
retrieved_docs.content: Potassium aminobenzoate, vitamin K 1, vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3 are all classified as vitamins. In addition, the compound described as “vitamin B1, plain and in combination with vitamin B6 and vitamin B12” is also a type of vitamin, and the plain form of vitamin B1 (thiamine) is a member of the thiamin family. Minerals are related to vitamins, and selenium is identified as a parent mineral within this group. Antifibrinolytic agents are linked to the vitamin K family: they are considered a subclass of vitamin K 1 and are also classified as vitamin K 2 and vitamin K 3. Moreover, antifibrinolytics belong to the broader hemostatic system, and the specific protein alfa‑2‑antiplasmin is an antifibrinolytic. This network of relationships highlights how certain vitamins (including various forms of vitamin K and vitamin B1) intersect with mineral biology and hemostasis, underscoring their combined relevance in nutritional physiology and coagulation management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 1 -- Community 39
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge base links the concept of application and dosage to a broad spectrum of pharmacologic agents, excipients and nutrients, emphasizing individualized dosing for safety and efficacy. Allowed qualifiers include estrogen antagonists (e.g., centchroman), antifibrinolytics, bile‑acid sequestrants such as cholestyramine resin, colestipol, colestipol hydrochloride and colesevelam, the prostaglandin E₁ analogue misoprostol, nifedipine, various vitamins (calcitriol, vitamin K (K₂, K₃), vitamin B₁ alone or combined with B₆ and B₁₂, a prenatal multivitamin tablet containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine HCl, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide), diclofenac, atorvastatin, dexlansoprazole, dipeptides (e.g., alanylglutamine), finasteride, cation‑ and anion‑exchange agents, ivermectin, deoxycholic acid,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 46
retrieved_docs.content: Multivitamin preparations are organized under the broad “vitaminy” category, which also includes vitamin IV solution additives, multivitamin + mineral preparations, prenatal multivitamin formulations (containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide and cupric oxide), and other complex vitamin‑mineral combinations. Individual vitamins are linked to “vitaminy” through hierarchical “isa”, “has parent”, “has child”, and “inverse_isa” relations: α‑tocopherol (vitamin E), vitamin K (including K₂ and K₃) as a parent for fat‑soluble vitamins, retinoids and vitamin A acetate (forms of vitamin A and vitamin D), and ascorbic acid (vitamin C) with its combination products (e.g., ascorbic acid + calcium). B‑vitamin groups are represented by the vitamin B complex, riboflavin, vitamin B₁ (alone or combined

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 50

These agents are combined in the prenatal‑plus multivitamin tablet (VITAMIN A ACETATE 2000 IU / BETA CAROTENE 2000 IU / ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 400 IU / α‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 22 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.84 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACINAMIDE 20 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 10 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12 µg / CALCIUM CARBONATE 200 mg / FERROUS FUMARATE 27 mg / ZINC OXIDE 25 mg / CUPRIC OXIDE 2 mg),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 98

Subclasses of vitaminy include **multivitamin preparations** such as “multivitamin agent,” “multivitamins with minerals,” and “multivitamin+mineral preparations,” which combine vitamins with essential minerals (calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide, cupric oxide, etc.). A representative prenatal tablet—VITAMIN A ACETATE 2000 IU / BETA‑CAROTENE 2000 IU / ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg / CHOLECALCIFEROL 400 IU / α‑TOCOPHEROL ACETATE 22 mg / THIAMINE MONONITRATE 1.84 mg / RIBOFLAVIN 3 mg / NIACINAMIDE 20 mg / PYRIDOXINE HYDROCHLORIDE 10 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / CYANOCOBALAMIN 12 µg / CALCIUM CARBONATE 200 mg / FERROUS

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 136
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “vitaminy” as a comprehensive class that encompasses individual vitamins (vitamin A, vitamin B₁, vitamin B₆, vitamin B₁₂, vitamin C [ascorbic acid], vitamin D, vitamin E [α‑tocopherol], vitamin K and its fat‑soluble subclasses including vitamin K₂/₃), their chemical derivatives, combination formulations, and related pharmaceutical preparations. Analogues and metabolites such as hydroxokobalamin, mecobalamin, retinoids, tocopherols, cholecalciferol, kalcitriol, and other derivatives are linked to their parent vitamins through “has parent” relationships, while compounds such as cholin are treated as child entities within the vitamin taxonomy. Multivitamin preparations, multivitamin agents, multivitamin IV‑solution additives, and complex multivitamin products (e.g., formulations containing vitamin A acetate, β‑carotene, ascorbic acid, cholecalciferol, α‑tocopherol acetate, thiamine mononitrate, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine hydrochloride, folic acid,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: E (α‑tocopherol) functions as an inactive ingredient in zinc pyrithione shampoos (3 mg/mL and 4.8 mg/mL) and is grouped with other fat‑soluble vitamins, whereas vitamin K formulations are used as IV additives to correct coagulopathy and remain distinct from retinoids. Vitamin C and its combinations (e.g., “ascorbic acid and calcium”) are placed in both “vitamin C products, plain” and “vitamin C combinations,” reflecting its dual role as a single nutrient and as part of combination therapies. Topical antipsoriatics are organized under the ATC subclass “antipsoriatics for topical use,” with specific agents such as antracen derivatives, tars, and “other antipsoriatics for topical use.” These categories are linked to undefined entities (“dehty”) and to a parent chemical concept (“كيمياء غير محدد”) that connects to laboratory impressions of folic acid + cyanocobalamin measured in serum or whole blood. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies how individual vitamins, their chemical forms,
